content.text:
Question:
                                I was going through our previous chat and I was wondering, what was Andy wearing in the script you wrote for the comedy movie scene?

                                Question date:
                                2023/05/25 (Thu) 14:10

                                Noisy expected-answer label:
                                Andy was wearing an untidy, stained white shirt.

                                Gold evidence:
                                ```text
                                LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/22 (Mon) 12:47
Transcript:
user: Write me the beginning of a script (formatted like a real movie script) for a comedy movie scene, based on the below information:

Andy, a man in his 40s, is the Head of Computing at a rural high school. He has a loud, deep voice, and has more confidence than he has intelligence. He wants to be friends with his colleague exam invigilator John, so often tries and fails to impress him with his elaborate stories. He often bangs on about how he presents the school talent show. He has poor personal hygiene and can often be found making moaning noises while using disabled toilets, despite him having no obvious disability. Andy wears an untidy, stained white shirt. When addressing a group he starts with the words "Right guys, settle down".

Roger, a man in his 50s, is a computing teacher working under Andy. He is an intelligent man, with grey hair and a laid back attitude to teaching. His classes are often out of control, while he sits there in despair. He has quiet contempt at the fact that Andy is the head of department, because he knows that he is much more qualified than Andy.

John is a 69 year old man working as an exam invigilator. As he is past retirement it is unusual that he is still working, but we suspect it might be because he enjoys making people uncomfortable. John has wispy, grey hair and wears a pinstripe suit, several gold rings and red tinted glasses. He makes brazen, inappropriate remarks towards his colleagues.

Ruth, a woman in her 40s, is a languages teacher at the high school. She is so obsessed with the German language and culture that she regularly speaks to people in German and they don't understand what she's saying. She is attracted to women and regularly has to deter John's advances.
assistant: INT. RURAL HIGH SCHOOL - COMPUTING DEPARTMENT - DAY

Andy, a man in his 40s with a loud, deep voice and poor personal hygiene, is standing in front of the department's whiteboard. He wears an untidy, stained white shirt.

ANDY:
Right guys, settle down. I just wanted to let you all know that this year's talent show is going to be bigger and better than ever. I'm putting in a lot of hard work to make sure it's a success.

ROGER:
(sitting in the back of the room)
Yeah, sure Andy. We all know how much work you put into the talent show.

ANDY:
(ignoring Roger's sarcasm)
And John, I know you'll be there to help me out as usual.

JOHN:
(69 year old man with wispy, grey hair and red tinted glasses)
I'll be there to make sure no one cheats, Andy.

RUTH:
(woman in her 40s, speaking in German)
Ja, John wird da sein, um sicherzustellen, dass niemand betrügt.

ANDY:
(confused)
Um, thanks Ruth. I'll make sure to brush up on my German before the talent show.

ROGER:
(rolling his eyes)
Good luck with that, Andy.

ANDY:
(determined)
I'll show you all that I'm a great talent show presenter. You just wait and see.

ROGER:
(under his breath)
I'll believe it when I see it.

The scene ends with Andy confidently walking out of the room, while the other teachers look on in amusement.
user: Write another scene
assistant: INT. RURAL HIGH SCHOOL - DISABLED TOILET - DAY

Andy is heard making moaning noises from inside the disabled toilet. He is not visibly disabled.

ROGER:
(knocking on the door)
Andy, are you in there?

ANDY:
(groaning)
Yeah, just a minute.

ROGER:
(sighing)
What are you doing in there?

ANDY:
(still moaning)
I don't know, I just felt like making some noise.

ROGER:
(exasperated)
Andy, you can't just use the disabled toilet for no reason. You're not disabled.

ANDY:
(defensive)
I'm the head of department, I can do what I want.

ROGER:
(shaking his head)
You need to get your act together, Andy.

The scene ends with Andy exiting the disabled toilet, looking sheepish.
user: Write another scene
assistant: INT. RURAL HIGH SCHOOL - STAFF ROOM - DAY

Andy is sitting at a table, trying to impress John with one of his elaborate stories.

ANDY:
...and then I was like, "Right guys, settle down" and they all listened to me. It was amazing.

JOHN:
(69 year old man with wispy, grey hair and red tinted glasses)
That's impressive, Andy.

RUTH:
(woman in her 40s, speaking in German)
Ja, das ist beeindruckend, Andy.

ANDY:
(confused)
Thanks, Ruth. I'm glad you enjoyed my story.

ROGER:
(rolling his eyes)
Andy, you always have the most interesting stories.

ANDY:
(defensive)
What's that supposed to mean?

ROGER:
(sarcastically)
It means that your stories are always exaggerated and not very believable.

ANDY:
(offended)
Well, maybe you just don't have a good sense of humor.

JOHN:
(making a lewd comment)
Maybe you just need to lighten up, Ruth.

RUTH:
(deterring John's advances)
John, please. I'm not interested.

The scene ends with Andy looking dejected and the other teachers awkwardly avoiding eye contact with him.
                                ```

                                Candidate answer:
                                Andy was wearing an untidy, stained white shirt in the comedy movie scene script.

reason.description: One short sentence explaining the judgement.

supported.description: Whether the candidate answer satisfies the expected answer.

systemPrompts:
You are an objective evaluator for a long-term-memory benchmark.
Decide whether the candidate answer correctly answers the question in the same core direction as the noisy expected-answer label.
The expected-answer label can contain noisy illustrative examples, broad inferences, or details not present in the gold evidence.
Use the gold evidence only to decide which expected-answer details are actually evidence-backed.
Mark supported=true when the candidate answer is equivalent to the evidence-backed central expected answer or contains all evidence-backed central facts needed to answer.
Mark supported=false when the candidate answer misses an evidence-backed central answer, contradicts it, leaves several plausible competing answers, or only answers a weaker unrelated subset.
Do not require expected-answer details that are absent from the gold evidence.
For count/list questions, independently enumerate the evidence-backed items from the gold evidence. If the expected numeric count is evidence-backed, mark supported=false when the candidate gives a different count or misses a counted evidence-backed item.
For count/list questions, judge evidence-backed item equivalence by category fit and explicit user attribution rather than exact verb wording. A different lifecycle or status verb can still support the answer when the gold evidence clearly places the item inside the requested category.
For acquisition-style broad category counts, first-person evidence of a concrete personal copy or item is enough to count that item unless the question asks for transaction details such as price, store, payment, download source, or exact date.
For broad category counts, do not require exact subtype words or exact title when ordinary language makes the evidence-backed personal item a member, copy, or instance of the requested category.
For broad counts of works or content items, count evidence-backed physical or digital carriers, copies, files, or personal items of that work as the work itself when user attribution is explicit.
For broad counts of music works or releases, count evidence-backed user-owned or downloaded vinyl records, CDs, cassettes, digital copies, and music downloads as album/EP/release items unless the evidence explicitly identifies them as only single tracks, playlists, non-audio merchandise, or the question asks for a narrower format or subtype.
For count/list questions, judge broad category labels as category-fit tests rather than exact-word qualifiers. Do not mark an answer unsupported only because the evidence names a member, carrier, copy, or concrete instance instead of repeating the category label.
For broad category counts, do not require an exact subtype word in evidence unless the evidence gives an explicit conflicting subtype/category or the question asks for exact subtype/transaction details.
For qualified recall questions, the candidate must satisfy every explicit qualifier supported by the gold evidence. Mark supported=false when it answers only a weaker partial match while another evidence-backed item satisfies the full qualified description.
For preference or personalization questions, supported=true when the candidate uses the relevant user-specific preference or fact in the same core direction.
For temporal questions, do not penalize off-by-one errors for counts of days, weeks, months, or similar durations.
For knowledge-update questions, the candidate may mention previous information if it also clearly gives the updated required answer.
Return only the configured JSON object.
